title: Swap & Duplicate
intro: No new commands — just famous combos. `xp` swaps two characters (delete one, paste it after the next), `ddp` swaps two lines, and `yyp` duplicates a line. They fall straight out of how delete, yank, and paste already work.
summary: xp, ddp, yyp — three tiny idioms you'll use forever.

exercises.instruction: Fix the classic typo: with the cursor on the `i`, `xp` turns `recieve` into `receive`.
hints: `x` deletes the `i`, `p` pastes it back one spot later.

exercises.instruction: The steps are in the wrong order: on the first line, `dd` then `p` swaps the two lines.
hints: `dd` cuts the line; `p` pastes it below the cursor.

exercises.instruction: Duplicate the line with `yyp`, ready to edit the copy.
hints: `yy` yanks linewise, so `p` pastes a whole new line.

exercises.instruction: Swap the middle and last lines: `ddp` from line 2.
hints: `ddp` works anywhere, not just at the top.

exercises.instruction: `teh` → `the`: put the cursor on the `e`, then `xp`.

hints: The cursor can be anywhere on the line for `yy`.
